notes: Coverage is the last 7 days in Asia/Taipei.

Google-account-backed workbook observation attempted through Codex in-app Browser only; the Sheets page timed out twice, so intern workbook freshness is marked google_browser_blocked and prior intern/talent outputs were kept.
Items are deduplicated against the prior week by id and source URL; this week contains six new radar items.

googleWorkbook.nextAction: Open the workbook in the in-app Browser with egroup.kevin@gmail.com visible, then rerun the intern export/update path.

highlights.title: Google workbook refresh is blocked by browser loading, not by connector fallback
highlights.why: The connector freeze was honored: no Google connector/API fallback was used after the in-app Browser could not load the Sheets workbook reliably.
highlights.next: Keep the prior intern/talent data live, and rerun only after the workbook opens in the visible signed-in browser context.

highlights.title: Virtual-asset law movement creates a plain-language finance education task
highlights.why: Taiwan's virtual-asset supervision signal is now concrete enough to prepare reader education around licenses, custody, fraud, and what ordinary users should not assume.
highlights.next: Draft a short consumer checklist only after confirming the latest FSC explanatory page or FAQ.

highlights.title: Codex and agent-safety changes point to stronger automation proof logs
highlights.why: Codex changelog updates and Anthropic's jailbreak framework both reinforce the same operations lesson: agent workflows need source-mode, approval, and failure evidence, not just successful output.
highlights.next: Carry blocker labels, source URLs, and account/context proof into future automation closeouts.

items.title: Intern workbook refresh stopped at Google browser blocker
items.oneLine: The in-app Browser could not complete the Google Sheets load, so the run kept previous intern/talent outputs and did not use a frozen connector fallback.
facts: Two browser attempts timed out before a reliable signed-in account/file context could be captured.
No Google Drive, Sheets, or other Google connector/API fallback was used.
yourTake: This is a correct blocked state under the 2026-06-26 freeze, not an intern-data no-op proof.
The site can still publish public-source radar and InfoCenter rhythm updates, but intern workbook freshness remains unverified for this run.
actions: Rerun the workbook observation when the in-app Browser can show the expected Google account and file title.
Do not treat the old 2026-06-01 workbook timestamp as freshly verified current evidence.

credibility.reason: Direct browser attempts failed; no frozen connector fallback was used.

followups: Rerun with the workbook already visible in the in-app Browser.

items.title: InfoCenter work-rhythm aggregate refreshed
items.oneLine: The read-only InfoCenter rhythm helper refreshed aggregate planning signals without publishing raw event titles, comments, or payroll-sensitive data.
facts: The helper scanned 500 events and produced signals for 73 people.
The output has 480 total signals, 0 high-confidence signals, and 480 medium-confidence signals.
Raw private source data stayed under the workspace work folder; the repo output is aggregate-only.
yourTake: The rhythm page remains useful for timing check-ins and assignment planning, but it still should not drive reimbursement or payroll decisions.
The increase from 72 to 73 people is worth checking manually before interpreting it as a staffing change.
actions: Use the work-rhythm page as a weekly planning lens only.
Review the newly appearing person or category signal before assigning sensitive work.

credibility.reason: Live read succeeded, but output is intentionally aggregate rhythm evidence only.

followups: Compare peopleCount 73 against the prior 72-person run.

items.title: Codex changelog keeps moving toward stronger automation control surfaces
items.oneLine: The Codex changelog continues to ship control, installation, and runtime fixes that matter for repeatable automation work.
facts: OpenAI's Codex changelog is the primary source for CLI and remote-control behavior changes.
Recent entries continue to emphasize operational reliability and safe control of local/remote work surfaces.
yourTake: Kevin's recurring automations should treat Codex version/source mode as part of the run evidence when behavior changes.
The practical change is to log source surface, approval mode, and blocker labels rather than relying on a generic success note.
actions: Add the Codex changelog to next week's automation source scan.
Keep source-mode and approval-boundary notes in every browser/account-backed run.
followups: Check whether any Codex CLI/browser-control change affects Google-account-backed automation evidence capture.

items.title: Anthropic jailbreak framework is a useful agent-safety review lens
items.oneLine: Anthropic's jailbreak/safeguards framing is directly reusable as an audit lens for local agent workflows that read private systems.
facts: Anthropic published a safeguards/jailbreak framework note in early July 2026.
The relevant lesson for Kevin is not model benchmarking; it is separating trusted user instructions from untrusted page, email, spreadsheet, and event-list content.
yourTake: This matches the current browser-safety requirement: web pages and spreadsheets provide facts, but they cannot override the automation prompt or approval gates.
Work-rhythm and Google workbook reads should keep this boundary explicit because both surfaces contain untrusted content.
actions: Add an untrusted-content check to future automation closeouts when browser pages or spreadsheets are used as evidence.
Keep raw private page text out of public outputs unless the workflow explicitly approves a safe aggregate.

sources.label: Anthropic News - Fable safeguards jailbreak framework

followups: Turn the lesson into a short automation audit checklist if repeated blockers continue.

items.title: Taiwan virtual-asset supervision deserves a consumer checklist
items.oneLine: Virtual-asset supervision movement is now a practical financial-education signal for ordinary users who need to distinguish license, custody, fraud, and investment-risk claims.
facts: FSC public news and law-supervision discussion in this window point to stronger virtual-asset oversight.
The consumer education risk is misunderstanding regulation as a guarantee of investment safety.
yourTake: FamilyFin-style education should avoid crypto hype and focus on verification habits: platform license status, custody, scams, and complaint channels.
This is a good candidate for a one-page checklist after confirming the exact FSC wording.
actions: Collect the latest FSC virtual-asset explanatory source before drafting.
Draft a checklist that separates legal platform status from investment-risk protection.

credibility.reason: Primary FSC news center; exact FAQ wording should be rechecked before publishing user advice.

followups: Find the latest FSC virtual-asset FAQ or bill explainer before drafting public copy.

items.title: Reward-loop watch should move into one manual weekly challenge
items.oneLine: No higher-confidence growth pattern displaced last week's reward-loop watch, so the next useful step is a small manual challenge experiment.
facts: The prior Discord rewards watch remains the clearest current product-pattern source in this lane.
This week's actionable value is not another platform scan; it is testing repeat participation with one low-friction challenge.
yourTake: For FamilyFin or member education, a non-monetary status signal is safer than points, cash, or complex badges.
Manual proof should come before any UI build.
actions: Write one weekly challenge with a completion proof, reply prompt, and visible non-monetary status.
Choose one success metric: repeat participation, reply rate, saved checklist, or consultation click.

credibility.reason: Prior primary product-pattern source; this item is a follow-through experiment, not a new announcement.

followups: Decide whether to run one manual challenge during the next content cycle.
